title: Birthright Citizenship Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for who automatically becomes a US citizen when they are born in America. Under the new rules, a baby born in the US would only become a citizen if at least one parent is a US citizen, a legal permanent resident, or a non-citizen legally serving in the military. Right now, almost all babies born in the US automatically become citizens regardless of their parents' status. The new rules would not change the citizenship of anyone who was already born before this law takes effect.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Birthright Citizenship Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Citizenship at Birth for Certain Persons Born in the United States
sections.text: This section changes the Immigration and Nationality Act, which is the main law that decides who becomes a US citizen. The changes add new rules about when babies born in the United States automatically become citizens. The law recognizes that the Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution gives birthright citizenship, which means people born here are usually citizens. However, this bill says that to be considered under US legal control for citizenship purposes, a baby born in the United States must have at least one parent who fits one of these descriptions. The parent must be a US citizen or US national. Or the parent must be a legal permanent resident who lives in the United States. Or the parent must be a non-citizen with legal status who is actively serving in the US military. These changes will not affect anyone who was already born before this law takes effect. Those people keep whatever citizenship status they already have.
